texts.orig: Membrane gas solvent contactors are a hybrid technology combining solvent absorption with membranes that enables efficient gas separation (Favre and Svendsen, 2012; Franco et al., 2008). One of the biggest advantages of membrane contactors is the high mass transfer area per unit volume, because of the highly efficient membrane module packing, which reduces the equipment size compared to solvent absorption (Falk-Pedersen et al., 2005; Reed et al., 1995). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas solvent contactors have the potential to revolutionize carbon capture, because the technology combines the advantages of both membrane and solvent technologies. Here, an asymmetric composite poly dimethylsiloxane (PDMS) on porous polysulfone membrane contactor was studied for the desorption of CO2 from loaded 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. Importantly, this study investigated the performance of the contactor at temperatures where the MEA solution entered the contactor as a liquid and as a vapor. It was found that the PDMS contactor CO2 flux was comparable to other reported membrane contactors when the MEA solution was in the solvent phase, but when the feed was vaporized the CO2 flux increased by an order of magnitude. Similarly, the overall mass transfer coefficient had the same behavior, in that an order of magnitude increase was obtained when the MEA solution was above the boiling temperature. The CO2 permeability through the non-porous PDMS layer was calculated based on mass transfer correlations and the temperature trend was comparable to literature. This indicated that CO2 transport through the PDMS layer was the same for the range of temperature and phase conditions studied, and that the mechanism was based on CO2 transporting in the gas phase. Significantly high water fluxes were observed through the PDMS membrane, two orders of magnitude greater than CO2, which was comparable with other non-porous contactors. However, at 110 ◦ C, the H2O/CO2 flux selectivity decreased to 14, indicative of the higher CO2 flux at that temperature because of the vapor feed.

Pore wetting can be minimized by utilizing hydrophobic polymeric membranes, the most common being polypropylene (PP) and poly tetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) (Chun and Lee, 1997; Hoff et al., 2004; Karoor and Sirkar, 1993; Kim and Yang, 2000; Mavroudi et al., 2003; Sengupta et al., 1998; Yeon et al., 2003). The choice of solvent is also important in terms of overall mass transfer and pore wetting, the most common being amines (mostly monoethanolamine (MEA)), amino acids and NaOH because of their use in traditional solvent absorption. Asymmetric non-porous membranes have been studied as membrane contactors for CO2 capture as an alternate approach, with varying success (Scholes et al., 2015a). For non-porous contactors the key issue relates to ensuring the non-porous layer is sufficiently thin to minimize mass transfer resistance while also being based on a high CO2 permeance polymer (Korikov and Sirkar, 2005). texts.label: text
texts.orig: The majority of membrane contactors for CCS have focused on CO2 absorption into the solvent, but membrane contactors can also be used for the desorption of CO2 from the loaded solvent for regeneration. Hence, there is potential to replace the desorption column in traditional solvent separation with a membrane contactor (Fig. 1). Previous studies have demonstrated that for CO2 desorption a number of non-porous contactors have similar performance to porous contactors. In particular, Wang et al. (2013) studied porous PP contactors for the desorption from a range of amine based solvents, including MEA, at 75 ◦ C, while Khaisri et al. (2011) studied porous PTFE contactors with MEA at 100 ◦ C and Simioni et al. (2011) studied porous PTFE contactors with 30 wt% potassium carbonate solvent over the 60-100 ◦ C temperature range. In all of these systems high CO2 fluxes were observed given the porous nature of the contactor, however solvent entry in the contactor and solvent cross-over to the gas phase were issues. Kosaraju et al. (2005) studied both a porous PP and an asymmetric PMP contactor for desorption from MEA and the non-volatile solvent PAMAM. They found that use of the non-volatile solvent significantly reduced pore wetting, while MEA could permeate the non-porous PMP structure. Trusov et al. (2011) studied three asymmetric contactors based on PTMSP, PMP and PTMGP for the stripping of CO2 from a range of solvents at 100 ◦ C. These contactors produced very high CO2 fluxes, in part because of the large pressure driving force across the contactor, given that the solvent was at 10 bar. This differs from other studies, which have operated the solvent at close to atmospheric pressure. Similarly, the authors have previously observed an increase in CO2 mass transfer with temperature when using asymmetric PTMSP, PIM-1 and Teflon AF1600 contactors for the desorption of CO2 from loaded MEA over the range 70-105 ◦ C (Scholes et al., 2015b). Critically, the high water flux dominates the mass transfer through these membrane contactors, given the high temperature required for CO2 desorption. As such, for asymmetric contactors it is imperative that the active layer be selective for CO2, while also being sufficiently hydrophobic to limit water mass transfer through the membrane, so as to minimize the downstream condenser duty. Furthermore, desorber contactor membrane studies have been limited to temperatures below the solvent boiling point, and hence the feed is in the liquid phase. However, operating above the boiling temperature ensures the feed is in the vapor phase and desorption operates similar to membrane distillation (Sirkar, 2001).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane contactor solvent regeneration apparatus has previously been reported in the literature (Khaisri et al., 2011; Scholes et al., 2015b). To ensure high flux through the membrane and prevent concentration polarization on the gas side, a N2 sweep gas is applied to the lumen side at ∼ 120 kPaa and with a flowrate of 1 L/min, controlled through a mass flow controller (Aalborg). The use of N2 would not be viable on an industrial scale, because of the need to generate a pure CO2 product. Hence, in these situations to ensure a high CO2 driving force across the membrane a vacuum could be applied to the permeate side, such as that applied by Fang et al. (2012), or alternatively low grade steam could be used as a sweep gas, with a condenser downstream removing the water. Solvent sampling was achieved by collecting the loaded solvent ( ∼ 2 mL) and analyzed through Chittick titration (Horowitz, 1974). At the four temperatures studied, the membrane contactors were operated for two hours to ensure steady-state conditions were achieved before CO2 and H2O fluxes were recorded. Long time studies were undertaken at 100 and 110 ◦ C, where CO2 flux was measured for twelve hours.

texts.orig: The CO2 flux through the asymmetric PDMS contactor as a function of solvent Reynolds number and temperature, after 2 h of operation, are provided in Fig. 2, with the 110 ◦ C temperature provided as the vapor phase Reynolds number. The CO2 flux increases with temperature because of the escalating driving force across the membrane, due to the increasing CO2 partial pressure above the solvent. The CO2 flux at 110 ◦ C is over an order of magnitude greater than those measured at lower temperatures, indicative of the change to the vapor phase in the feed stream and the full conversion of carbamate to CO2 that occurs (Wilcox, 2012). texts.label: text
texts.orig: A critical aspect of solvent absorption for carbon capture is the economics of the process and the need to reduce the energy duty of solvent regeneration. While operating the membrane contactor at 110 ◦ C achieves a significant CO2 flux and reduces the required contactor area, the energy duty increases substantially because of the need to supply the latent heat of vaporization of the solvent. Hence, there exists a trade-off between CO2 flux through the membrane contactor and the energy duty of the process. An optimal operating temperature will exist that balances this energy duty against the CO2 flux (membrane area), which will be dependent upon the economics of the overall process.

texts.orig: 4.2. Overall mass transfer coefficient
texts.text: 4.2. Overall mass transfer coefficient

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficient for CO2 desorption through the asymmetric PDMS membrane contactor, averaged for the Reynolds numbers studied, is provided in Fig. 3. Similar to the CO2 flux, the overall mass transfer coefficient increases with temperature, indicative of the increased CO2 diffusivity and reduced solvent viscosity lowering the mass transfer resistance associated with the solvent boundary layer. The order of magnitude increase in mass transfer coefficient observed at 110 ◦ C is due to the vapor phase on the feed side of the contactor, which substantially reduces the mass transfer resistance of the feed side boundary layer. The overall mass transfer coefficients for the PDMS membrane contactor, below 110 ◦ C, are of a similar order of magnitude to those reported in the literature for other asymmetric composite membrane contactors (Scholes et al., 2015b), operating under the same solvent temperature, CO2 loading conditions and similar Reynolds numbers. The calculated permeabilities are of the same magnitude as those reported from gas permeability measured, though on average lower by ∼ 400 barrer. This difference is attributed to the presence of water and MEA that will sorb into the PDMS structure and compete with CO2 with the polymeric matrix (Chen et al., 2012; Scholes et al., 2012b). Indeed, MEA permeation through the PDMS membrane contactor was observed at all temperatures studied, highlighting that loss of MEA from the solvent will occur during regeneration. It was not possible to accurately measure the flux of MEA through the contactor, but it is expected to increase with temperature. This permeation of MEA through the PDMS contactor supports that observed by Kosaraju et al. (2005) for MEA permeation through an asymmetric PMP contactor.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Water flux
texts.text: 4.3. Water flux

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The water fluxes through the asymmetric PDMS contactor are provided in Fig. 5, as a function of temperature, averaged over the Reynolds numbers studied. For all four temperatures studied, the water flux is greater than the CO2 flux (irrespective of Reynolds number, Fig. 2), indicative that under desorption conditions the membrane contactor has selectivity for water over CO2 (Fig. 6). The increase in water flux with temperature is caused by the substantial increase in the water driving force across the membrane because of the increase in water vapor pressure. Similar water flux behavior has been observed for other non-porous membrane contactors (Scholes et al., 2015b), and is analogous to membrane distillation processes (Sirkar, 2001).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The H2O/CO2 flux selectivity is clearly above 240 (Fig. 6), for temperatures below 110 ◦ C. This is similar behavior to that observed for other non-porous polymeric membranes and a water selectivity of these magnitudes are standard for many polymeric membranes (Scholes et al., 2009). The H2O/CO2 selectivity increase between 90 and 105 ◦ C is attributed to the water flux increasing by a greater proportion relative to the CO2 flux because of the increase in the respective driving forces across the membrane. At 110 ◦ C, the H2O/CO2 falls significantly to 14, and while this still favors H2O over CO2, the behavior highlights the significant increase in CO2 traversing the membrane when the feed is in the vapor phase.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4. Long time study
texts.text: 4.4. Long time study

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The change in CO2 flux through the asymmetric PDMS contactor over time is provided in Fig. 7 for 100 and 110 ◦ C. The fluxes are represented as a percentage relative to the two hour measurement, which is the basis of the results presented above. The results clearly demonstrate that for both temperatures there is a small continual increase in CO2 flux with time; after 8 h of operation the flux has increased by ∼ 2.5% over the two hour values reported above for both temperatures, and after 12 h of operation the flux has increased by ∼ 7% for both temperatures. Hence, pseudo steady-state conditions are obtained relatively quickly in the operation, but on a longer time scale an underlying slow phenomenon is inducing change in the contactor system. This increase in flux is the opposite of that observed for membrane contactors undergoing CO2 absorption, where gradual pore wetting leads to a loss in CO2 flux (Franco et al., 2008). Hence, pore wetting is not an issue for desorption of CO2 at these temperatures. A possible reason for the slow increase in CO2 flux is attributed to the high temperature of the process and high water and MEA vapor pressures inducing structural change within the polymeric membrane contactor. In particular, pore swelling and plasticization may increase the free volume of both the PDMS layer and porous polysulfone support. Such behavior has been observed in polymeric gas separation membranes exposed to water vapor (Chen et al., 2011). MEA permeating the contactor may also be degrading the polysulfone support layer, given the high temperatures of operation. Alternatively, the

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Asymmetric PDMS on a porous polysulfone support membrane contactors were studied for CO2 desorption from loaded 30 wt% MEA at a range of temperatures and feed Reynolds numbers. It was found that the CO2 flux was an order of magnitude greater when the feed was in the vapor phase compared to being in the solvent. This was attributed to the difference in driving force across the membrane, which was associated with the CO2 partial pressure above the loaded solvent. The overall mass transfer coefficient was determined and for temperatures at and below 105 ◦ C the behavior was comparable to other asymmetric contactor systems, but less than those observed for porous contactors. At 110 ◦ C, the overall mass transfer coefficient was substantially increased because of the change in state. The permeability of CO2 within the non-porous PDMS layer was calculated from the overall mass transfer coefficient and found to be lower when compared to that reported in the literature for PDMS. This was attributed to competitive sorption of water and MEA sorbed within the PDMS layer. The water flux through the asymmetric PDMS contactor was always greater than CO2. Interestingly, the CO2 flux slightly increased when studied over a long time period, which was attributed to the high temperature conditions altering the membrane morphology and/or weakening the adhesive seal.
texts.text: Asymmetric PDMS on a porous polysulfone support membrane contactors were studied for CO2 desorption from loaded 30 wt% MEA at a range of temperatures and feed Reynolds numbers. It was found that the CO2 flux was an order of magnitude greater when the feed was in the vapor phase compared to being in the solvent. This was attributed to the difference in driving force across the membrane, which was associated with the CO2 partial pressure above the loaded solvent. The overall mass transfer coefficient was determined and for temperatures at and below 105 ◦ C the behavior was comparable to other asymmetric contactor systems, but less than those observed for porous contactors. At 110 ◦ C, the overall mass transfer coefficient was substantially increased because of the change in state. The permeability of CO2 within the non-porous PDMS layer was calculated from the overall mass transfer coefficient and found to be lower when compared to that reported in the literature for PDMS. This was attributed to competitive sorption of water and MEA sorbed within the PDMS layer. The water flux through the asymmetric PDMS contactor was always greater than CO2. Interestingly, the CO2 flux slightly increased when studied over a long time period, which was attributed to the high temperature conditions altering the membrane morphology and/or weakening the adhesive seal.
